title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Overview
This short book looks at the Cambridge Central School District (Cambridge CSD, Washington County, New York) alongside neighboring districts, using public data from the New York State Education Department (NYSED). It is one of several analyses in the ccs project; the aim is to put Cambridge’s numbers in context rather than to view them in isolation.

title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Comparison groups
Different questions call for different peer sets, so the book uses two groups that can be adjusted as the work develops:

Graph group (5 districts) — used for charts, where a short list keeps lines legible: Cambridge, Greenwich, Salem (all Washington County), Schuylerville (Saratoga County), and Hoosick Falls (Rensselaer County). These are Cambridge’s closest small-district neighbors.
Table group (12 districts) — used for tables, which can hold more rows: every Washington County K-12 district plus the two graph-group districts that sit just outside the county (Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls).

The table group is the broader set; the graph group is a readable subset of it. Putnam CSD is left out of the “Washington County K-12” set because it is a K-8 district that tuitions out its high-schoolers (zero students in grades 9–12), so it is not comparable on K-12 measures.







Group
Districts




Graph (charts)
Cambridge, Greenwich, Salem, Schuylerville, Hoosick Falls


Table (tables)
The graph group + Argyle, Fort Ann, Fort Edward, Granville, Hartford, Hudson Falls, Whitehall

title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Data and a few cautions
The underlying numbers come from NYSED’s public downloads; each chapter and the Sources appendix give the specifics. A few things to keep in mind throughout:

Year labels are school-year-ending years. “2025” means the 2024–25 school year. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count (first Wednesday of October).
Teacher counts begin in 2017–18. So time series that depend on teachers (such as students per teacher) start at school-year-ending 2018, even though enrollment runs back to 2015–16.
Students per teacher is an approximation, not official class size. It divides K-12 enrollment by a district-wide teacher headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents). It is useful for comparison across districts and over time, but it is not NYSED’s official pupil-teacher or average-class-size figure. See the Sources appendix for detail.
Charter schools are excluded — they have no district-total row in the source data — so these are district-operated figures.

title: 1  Class sizes and staffing
text:
1.1 Students per teacher, over time
How many students does each teacher serve, and how has that changed? This chapter starts with the broadest staffing measure available across districts — K-12 students per teacher — and compares Cambridge with its neighbors over time. Later sections will add related measures (and, where NYSED publishes it, official average class size).
A reminder from the Overview: this ratio divides K-12 enrollment by a district-wide teacher headcount, so it is an approximate comparison measure, not an official class-size statistic. Lower values mean fewer students per teacher.
The chart below tracks the graph group from school-year-ending 2018 (the first year teacher counts exist) through 2025.
Cambridge now has the fewest students per teacher of the group.
Code
g = cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)  # 5 districts, 2018+

pdf = (
    g.select("district", "year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher")
    .drop_nulls()
    .to_pandas()
)
pdf["district"] = pd.Categorical(
    pdf["district"], categories=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP), ordered=True
)
cambridge = pdf[pdf["district"] == cc.FOCUS]

(
    ggplot(pdf, aes("year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(values=cc.PALETTE, name="")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="K–12 students per teacher",
        # Title states only the facts; the interpretive headline lives above the
        # figure in the prose (see the project's graph-title convention).
        title="District K–12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount, 2017–18 to 2024–25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.1: K–12 students per teacher by school year ending, 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25). Lower means fewer students per teacher. Source: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases.
In 2017–18 only Salem had a lower ratio than Cambridge. Cambridge’s ratio then drifted down — its K-12 enrollment fell about 15% (from 869 to 741) while its teacher headcount stayed roughly flat — and by the two most recent years Cambridge has the lowest students-per-teacher ratio of the five. A jump in reported teachers in 2023–24 pushed its 2024 figure especially low (8.2). Schuylerville and Greenwich actually fell more over the full period (about 2.0 and 1.9, versus Cambridge’s 1.5), but from much higher starting points, so they remain above Cambridge.

title: 1  Class sizes and staffing
text:
1.2 The full comparison group
The table widens the lens to all twelve table-group districts. Columns are school-years-ending; the final column is the change from 2018 to 2025 (a negative value means fewer students per teacher than in 2018). Cambridge is highlighted.


Code
t = cc.panel_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP)

# Long -> wide (years become columns), Washington County districts first,
# then alphabetical within each region.
wide = (
    t.select("region", "district", "year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher")
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(
        values="k12_students_per_teacher",
        index=["_rank", "region", "district"],
        on="year_end",
    )
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide = wide.with_columns(
    (pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2018")).alias("chg")
)

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="K–12 students per teacher",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25)",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols + ["chg"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2018→2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYSED Enrollment database (K-12 enrollment) and "
            "Student & Educator database (teacher counts). "
            "Ratio = district K-12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount; an "
            "approximate comparison measure, not official class size. "
            "Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)

Source: NYSED Enrollment database (K-12 enrollment) and Student & Educator database (teacher counts). Ratio = district K-12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount; an approximate comparison measure, not official class size. Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25).












Across the wider group, Cambridge ends among the lowest ratios — close to Salem — while the rest of Washington County’s K-12 districts span a wider range. The table is also a reminder that these districts differ greatly in size (compare small districts like Fort Edward with larger ones like Hudson Falls and Granville), which shapes how much a single hire or a small enrollment swing moves the ratio.

title: Sources & references
text:
NYSED data files
All figures in this book come from public data. This appendix documents each source, notes the main caveats, and lists full references (APA style) at the end. Citation keys are given in brackets so they can be reused in the text.
The enrollment and staffing numbers are built from two New York State Education Department databases, both downloaded from https://data.nysed.gov/downloads.php and distributed as annual Microsoft Access files:
These were stacked into a district-by-year panel by src/build_enrollment_teachers.py; the dataset’s own README records the build in full.
Caveats worth repeating. num_teachers is a district-wide headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents), while k12_enrollment is K-12 only. The derived students-per-teacher ratio is therefore an approximate measure for comparison across districts and over time — not NYSED’s official pupil-teacher ratio or average class size. Charter schools are excluded (they have no district-total row), and a small number of districts reorganized within the window (statewide, not in this comparison group).

title: Sources & references
text:
Enrollment database (New York State Education Department, 2025a) — the BEDS Day Enrollment table, which gives K-12 enrollment by district. Coverage in this book: school years 2015–16 through 2024–25. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count (first Wednesday of October).
Student & Educator (“STUDED”) database (New York State Education Department, 2025b) — the Staff table, which gives teacher, principal, counselor, and social-worker counts plus teacher attendance and turnover. Coverage: school years 2017–18 through 2024–25. Because teacher counts start in 2017–18, any measure that uses teachers (such as students per teacher) begins at school-year-ending

title: Sources & references
text:
Cambridge CSD annual financial reports
Cambridge’s audited annual financial statements, for fiscal years ended June 30, 2020 through June 30, 2025, were retrieved from the Federal Audit Clearinghouse (https://app.fac.gov/). The clearinghouse was operated by the U.S. Census Bureau through about 2022 and by the U.S. General Services Administration from 2023 onward — which is why the earlier files (FY2020–FY2022) carry a CENSUS tag and bear the title “Financial statements and independent auditor’s report,” while the later files (FY2023–FY2025) carry a GSAFAC tag and the title “Financial report.” They are the same series of district audits.

FY2020 (Cambridge Central School District, 2020), FY2021 (Cambridge Central School District, 2021), FY2022 (Cambridge Central School District, 2022)
FY2023 (Cambridge Central School District, 2023), FY2024 (Cambridge Central School District, 2024), FY2025 (Cambridge Central School District, 2025)

title: Sources & references
text:
Reader comments
Each page of this book has a comment area at the bottom, powered by Giscus. To leave a comment or a reaction, sign in with a free GitHub account; comments are stored as discussions in this book’s GitHub repository.
